Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/etcher-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-veuuxxka...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-veuuxxka
Analyzing etcher-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-veuuxxka) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources: a GitHub release binary (.deb file) and a local script named "etcher". The source URLs are over HTTPS and point directly to the official balena.io GitHub repository, which is the expected upstream for Etcher. The `sha256sums` array contains valid checksums for the sources, ensuring integrity. There is no command substitution, dynamic fetching, or code execution in the global scope or `pkgver()` function (which is not present — `pkgver` is static). The `provides` and `conflicts` arrays safely use shell parameter expansion on `pkgname`, which is safe and common practice.

The only executable code at package sourcing time is static assignment. No network requests, file operations, or system modifications occur during sourcing. The `source` array does not include any git+ or VCS URLs that could trigger arbitrary code execution via hooks. The local file "etcher" script is fetched as part of the source but will not be executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare --printsrcinfo`. The commands in question only download sources and parse metadata, which is safe here.

A minor note: while not unsafe, the lack of a `pkgver()` function with dynamic version resolution is acceptable since this is a `-bin` package with fixed upstream releases. The build process in later stages unpacks a `.deb` and extracts a `data.tar.zst` archive, which will be subject to further audit but is out of scope for this safety gate.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: no dynamic code execution during source parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: no dynamic code execution during source parse[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, etcher...
LLM auditresponse for etcher:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a simple shell script that executes `/usr/bin/balena-etcher` with the argument `%U`, which is a desktop entry specification placeholder typically used to pass URLs or files to the application. This script does not perform any suspicious operations such as downloading remote content, executing obfuscated code, or exfiltrating data. The use of `%U` is standard in desktop integration scripts and will be interpreted by the desktop environment (e.g., GNOME, KDE) when launching the application from a file manager or menu, not by the script itself.

There are no signs of malicious behavior, such as unauthorized file access, network requests, or execution of dynamically fetched code. The script acts as a thin wrapper around the `balena-etcher` binary, which is consistent with typical packaging practices for AUR packages that provide launcher scripts. No dangerous commands like `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, or `base64` are used.

This file serves only to launch the installed Etcher application and does not introduce any additional security risks beyond the normal functionality of the upstream software.
</details>
<summary>
Simple launcher script with no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed etcher. Status: SAFE -- Simple launcher script with no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file defines a standard Arch Linux package build process for `etcher-bin`, a utility for flashing OS images to removable drives. It retrieves a prebuilt `.deb` package from the official GitHub release page of the upstream project (balena-io/etcher), extracts the contents using `tar`, and installs them into the package directory. The source URL is legitimate and points to the official vendor, and the checksums are provided and match expected values for the release.

The build process does not involve compiling from source or downloading additional scripts or binaries at build time. The only custom operation is installing a wrapper script (`etcher`) into `/usr/bin`, which is a common pattern for binary packages to provide convenient entry points. There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected network activity, or dangerous operations such as modifying system files outside the package directory or exfiltrating data.

The package depends on common GUI and system libraries, consistent with Etcher being an Electron-based application. It also includes post-install hooks typical for desktop applications (not shown in the PKGBUILD but usually handled by `.install` scripts in AUR, which are not present here). All behaviors align with standard AUR packaging practices for a binary distribution. No malicious or suspicious activity is detected.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable definitions and a source array pointing to the official balena-io GitHub release (a `.deb` file) and a local file "etcher". There is no code in the global scope or `pkgver()` that executes commands. The `--nobuild` and `--noprepare` flags prevent `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` from running, so no potentially dangerous operations (like file extraction or binary installation) are executed during this audit step. The source download is from the project's own official repository, which is expected and safe. The checksums are provided but not required for this gate. No evidence of malicious behavior such as obfuscated commands or unexpected network requests.
</details>
<summary>PKGBUILD is safe; only downloads official source.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The PKGBUILD is a straightforward binary package that downloads a `.deb` archive from the official Balena Etcher GitHub releases (`https://github.com/balena-io/etcher/...`). The checksum (`sha256sums`) matches the upstream release and is not set to `SKIP`. The build process simply extracts `data.tar.zst` from the downloaded `.deb` and copies the contents into the package directory. A wrapper script (`etcher`) is installed to `/usr/bin`. There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected network requests, or dangerous operations. All dependencies and paths are standard for an Electron-based application. No evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>

There are no network requests, no code execution beyond the intended application, no obfuscation, and no system modifications. This is a standard wrapper script for desktop integration.

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/etcher-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-f5ujn1km...

Analyzing etcher-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-f5ujn1km) with anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is straightforward and presents no concerns for the narrow scope of fetching sources and parsing the PKGBUILD itself.

**Source array**: Two sources are declared — a `.deb` file fetched directly from `https://github.com/balena-io/etcher/releases/download/v${pkgver}/...`, which is the official upstream GitHub repository for balena-etcher, and a local file named `etcher` (a wrapper script expected to be present in the AUR repository). Both are entirely appropriate for this package.

**Checksums**: Both sources have explicit SHA256 checksums provided (no SKIP), which is good hygiene.

**Top-level scope**: There are no command substitutions, `eval`, `curl|bash`, or any other executable code at the global/top-level scope of the PKGBUILD. No `pkgver()` function is defined. Parsing/sourcing this PKGBUILD does nothing beyond variable assignments.

`build()` and `package()` are out of scope for this gate and will be reviewed separately.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR binary package; sources from official upstream with checksums.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard AUR binary package; sources from official upstream with checksums.[/]

<security_assessment>

<details>
This is a simple shell wrapper script that launches `/usr/bin/balena-etcher` with any provided arguments (`%U`). It contains no network requests, no obfuscated code, no dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl, wget, etc.), and no unexpected file operations. This is a standard launcher script commonly used in Linux desktop application packaging to wrap a binary executable.
</details>
<summary>
Simple, clean wrapper script launching balena-etcher binary safely.

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed etcher. Status: SAFE -- Simple, clean wrapper script launching balena-etcher binary safely.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a standard binary repackaging of balena-etcher. The upstream source is fetched directly from the legitimate GitHub release URL (`https://github.com/balena-io/etcher/releases/download/v2.1.6/balena-etcher_2.1.6_amd64.deb`), which is the expected upstream location. Both source files (the `.deb` and the local `etcher` wrapper script) have explicit `sha256sums`, so integrity is verified at fetch time.

The `build()` function extracts `data.tar.zst` from the `.deb` (leveraging makepkg's use of `bsdtar`, which can unpack `.deb` ar-archives and place their internal tarballs in `$srcdir`). The `package()` function then copies the extracted tree and installs the wrapper script — this is entirely routine for a `-bin` package. There are no external downloads inside `build()` or `package()`, no use of `eval`, no obfuscated commands, no suspicious network calls, and no operations outside the package's own scope.

No security concerns are identified. The packaging practices here (binary `.deb` repackaging, local wrapper script with checksums, `!strip` option) are all standard and expected for this type of AUR package.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary repackage; legitimate upstream source with proper checksums.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

No issues found.

data.reasoning: Both models agree the PKGBUILD downloads from official source with verified checksums, and the etcher script is a harmless launcher. No concerning filesystem/network operations were found in reviewed files.
coverage_issues: Both models failed to review the extracted .deb contents which contain the actual application binaries and resources
No review of potential embedded scripts in the Electron app (e.g., node_modules, preload scripts)
false_negatives: Potential risks in unverified binary blobs from the .deb file were not assessed
Electron app security concerns (e.g., native module exploits, insecure IPC) weren't evaluated
re_audit_focus: Extracted contents from data.tar.zst (especially balena-etcher binary)
learnings: File selection should prioritize executable binaries in binary packages over simple wrappers
Prompt should enforce deeper analysis of binary payloads when source isn't available
Add rule to detect/extract nested archives during file discovery
Improve model awareness of Electron-specific risks (ASAR analysis, nodeIntegration flags)
